coredata.dc:title: One-pot solvothermal synthesis of carbon dots/Ag nanoparticles/TiO2 nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic performance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chunlai
Yang, Kun
Wei, Xiaohui
Ding, Sheng
Tian, Feng
Li, Fan
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we reported a “green” and facile method for one-pot solvothermal synthesis of carbon dots (CDs)/Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs)/titanium dioxide (TiO2, commercial Degussa P25) ternary nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic performance. The characterizations of this ternary photocatalyst were studied at length and our results revealed that the crystalline phase of TiO2 component remained unchanged after the reaction. While the newborn AgNPs and CDs were tightly attached onto the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. The photocatalytic activities of photocatalysts were tested by measurements of photo-degradation on methylene blue (MB) under ultraviolet (UV) and visible light. It was showed that the photocatalytic performance of the ternary photocatalyst was superior to that of single TiO2 or CDs/TiO2 binary photocatalyst. It was probably attributed to the synergistic effect of the photoelectrical properties of CDs and the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of AgNPs, which could both enhance the absorption of visible light and hinder the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Nanocomposites
TiO2
Photocatalytic performance
Methylene blue (MB)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic procedure for the synthesis of CDs/Ag/P25 photocatalyst.
XRD patterns of P25, CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25.
Raman spectra of P25, CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25.
The SEM images of (a) CDs/P25 and (b) CDs/Ag/P25; (c), (d) and (e) TEM images of CDs/Ag/P25; (f) EDS energy spectrum of CDs/Ag/P25.
The XPS spectra of the CDs/Ag/P25 photocatalyst (a) Full survey; (b) Ag 3d spectra; (c) C 1s spectra; (d) O 1s spectra.
The photocatalytic activities and the linear fittings of the pseudo-first-order kinetics model of photocatalysts in degrading MB under (a-b) UV and (c-d) visible light.
The diffuse reflectance spectra and photographs of P25, CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25.
The PL spectra of P25, CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25 under the excitation of 320 nm.

Control experiments by using different radical scavengers for degradation of MB over CDs/Ag/P25 photocatalyst under UV light for 15 min (a) reaction in the absence of any radical scavengers (Initial); reaction with (b) isopropanol (IPA), (c) ammonium oxalate (AO) and (d) benzoquinone (BQ) as a scavenger for hydroxyl radicals (•OH), photogenerated holes (h+) and superoxide radical species (•O2 −), respectively.
The possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of CDs/Ag/P25 photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: One-pot solvothermal synthesis of carbon dots/Ag nanoparticles/TiO2 nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Medical Support Technology, Academy of Military Sciences, Tianjin 300161, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Medical Support Technology, Academy of Military Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we reported a “green” and facile method for one-pot solvothermal synthesis of carbon dots (CDs)/Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs)/titanium dioxide (TiO2, commercial Degussa P25) ternary nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic performance. The characterizations of this ternary photocatalyst were studied at length and our results revealed that the crystalline phase of TiO2 component remained unchanged after the reaction. While the newborn AgNPs and CDs were tightly attached onto the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. The photocatalytic activities of photocatalysts were tested by measurements of photo-degradation on methylene blue (MB) under ultraviolet (UV) and visible light. It was showed that the photocatalytic performance of the ternary photocatalyst was superior to that of single TiO2 or CDs/TiO2 binary photocatalyst. It was probably attributed to the synergistic effect of the photoelectrical properties of CDs and the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of AgNPs, which could both enhance the absorption of visible light and hinder the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the semiconductor photocatalysts have attracted considerable attention to solve current environmental and energy issues [1]. In particular, titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been commonly recognized as one of the most promising photocatalysts because of its excellent photocatalytic activity, relatively low cost, long-term stability and low toxicity [2–4]. Nonetheless, the low visible light responsivity of TiO2 owing to its large band gap (3.2 eV) and the fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs restrict its applications in photocatalysis [5,6]. A great deal of approaches have been developed to modify TiO2 so as to improve its photo-response capability, such as modification on TiO2 crystal [7] or morphology [8], sensitization with quantum dot [9] or organic dye [10,11], noble metal deposition [12–14], doping with metal [15,16] or nonmetal elements [17,18], coupling with the other semiconductors [19,20].

Among the above approaches, sensitization with quantum dot refers to the combination of TiO2 and quantum dots. It can adjust the band width of TiO2 and broaden the photo-response range. However, the traditional quantum dots often contain toxic heavy metal ions constituents, which is a serious threat to the environment and human health [21]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of carbon nanomaterial, are composed of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms. And the surface of CDs usually has abundant functional groups. Compared with the traditional quantum dots, CDs are superior in good biocompatibility, low toxicity, high aqueous solubility, easily functionalized modifications and chemical inertness [22,23]. In addition, CDs exhibit excellent photoelectrical properties including favorable electron transport and reservoir capability, photoluminescence (PL) and wide light absorption [24]. Some CDs with up-conversion PL property can absorb long-wave light and then emit short-wave light, which would further help to enhance the light utilization [25]. It has been found that semiconductors combined with CDs can enhance the light absorption and promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [26–28]. To date, many researches on CDs/TiO2 composites have been explored. And TiO2 with various microstructures were used to combine with CDs, including nanoparticles [29], nanofibers [30,31], nanotubes [32,33], nanorods [34], nanosheets [35] and nanobelts [36,37]. However, the current synthesis routines usually consisted of two parts: (1) the synthesis of CDs; (2) the combination of CDs and TiO2 using solvothermal treatment [38], electrochemistry deposition [39] or physical mixing method [40]. The complex synthesis procedures are still far from satisfactory. Additionally, though the light absorption of CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites in the visible and even the near infrared light region is enhanced, it is still weak for practical applications compared with that in ultraviolet (UV) light region, which needs further improvements all the time.

The noble metal nanoparticles (e.g. Au, Ag and Pt) are often used to improve the photocatalytic activities of semiconductor photocatalysts since they can act as irreversible electron sinks to enhance the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Among these noble metal nanoparticles, Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) possess a relatively lower price than the others. And the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of AgNPs could also help semiconductor photocatalysts to extend their light absorption to long-wave regions [41,42]. Furthermore, AgNPs can provide abundant chemically active sites where relevant chemical transformations would take effect with lower activation barriers than that on the semiconductors.

Considering the excellent photocatalytic activity of TiO2, the photoelectrical properties of CDs and the SPR effect of AgNPs, we think that the combination of components may be a good system with more efficient photocatalytic performance. CDs could be synthesized by using ethylene glycol (EG) as the carbon source through a solvothermal method [43,44]. Herein, we designed a one-pot solvothermal method for the fabrication of ternary nanocomposites. The possible schematic procedure was illustrated in Fig. 1. Typically, EG was used as the carbon source. TiO2 nanoparticles (Degussa P25) were added to EG followed by the addition of Ag ions under vigorous stirring. Then the mixture solution was heated at high temperature. Finally, CDs were synthesized through a solvothermal method. Meanwhile, Ag ions in the solution were reduced to AgNPs due to the reducing capacity of CDs [45,46]. Therefore, it was fabricated without the assistance of additional chemicals or special reducing agent. And AgNPs and CDs were tightly attached onto the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles through the solvothermal process.

P25, a commercial TiO2 nanoparticles (20% rutile and 80% anatase), was purchased from Degussa. Ethylene glycol (EG), silver nitrate (AgNO3), dehydrated alcohol, methylene blue (MB) were obtained from GuangFu Technology Development Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Ammonium oxalate (AO), isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and benzoquinone (BQ) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents were analytical grade reagents and used as received without further treatment. The deionized water used was from local source.

Photocatalysts were synthesized by using EG and AgNO3 as the carbon source and the silver source, respectively. In detail, 1.0 g P25 was added to 25 mL EG. The mixture was stirred vigorously for 5 h to obtain a homogeneous suspension. Then, 50 mg AgNO3 was added to the suspension with vigorous stirring in the dark to make the precursor solution. After that, the precursor solution was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 5 h. The ternary nanocomposites (named as CDs/Ag/P25) were collected by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and dehydrated alcohol repeatedly, and then dried in vacuum at 40 °C overnight. For comparison, the binary nanocomposites (named as CDs/P25) were prepared under similar reaction conditions except the addition of AgNO3.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were characterized by a Rigaku-D/MAX 2500 diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) with CuKα radiation (λ = 0.15405 nm) at the scanning speed of 2°/min; Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained by using a JEM 2010FEF microscopy (JEOL, Japan) at 200 kV; The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images and energy dispersive X-ray (EDS) elemental maps were carried out by 1530 VP microscope (LEO, Germany) at the accelerating voltage of 15 kV; Raman spectra were conducted on a RM 2000 Raman microscope (Renishaw, England) with 514 nm laser as the excitation source; The inverted fluorescence microscope images were measured by using an inverted microscope (NIKON Ti-S, Japan) in bright field, ultraviolet light, blue light and green light; X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed with EscaLab 250 Xi system (Thermo Fisher, USA) with monochromatic AlKα radiation; The UV–vis spectra were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1700, Japan) over the range of λ = 200–700 nm; The diffuse reflectance spectra were characterized by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600 plus, Japan) with BaSO4 as the reflectance standard material; The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were measured by using a TriStar-3020 surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics, USA) after being degassed in vacuum at 160 °C for 6 h. The pore-size distributions were obtained from the desorption branch of the isotherm of the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model. And the total specific surface areas were calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equation. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm.

The photocatalytic activities of photocatalysts were evaluated by the degradation of MB solution. P25, CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25 were tested under the same conditions for comparison. For the photo-degradation tests under UV light, 8 W UV lamp was used with a distance about 10 cm from the reaction vial. Typically, 20 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL MB solution (10 mg/L). Before illumination, the solution was stirred in the dark for 30 min to ensure the establishment of adsorption-desorption equilibrium between MB and the photocatalyst surface. During the irradiation, 2 mL solution was sampled at regular time intervals and centrifuged to remove photocatalyst. The supernatant was analyzed by a UV–vis spectrophotometer for calculating the degradation rate of MB. For the photo-degradation test under visible light, the light source was an incandescent lamp with a UV cut-off filter of 420 nm. And 50 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL MB solution (10 mg/L). The other steps of the experiment were consistent with the photo-degradation test under UV light.

To detect the main active species, the control experiments were carried out with the addition of different free radical scavengers. IPA, AO and BQ were served as a scavenger for hydroxyl radicals (•OH), photogenerated holes (h+) and superoxide radical species (•O2 −), respectively [47,48]. And the experiments were in a similar way as the former photo-degradation measurement.

The XRD patterns can provide direct evidences for the phase structures of materials. As shown in Fig. 2, P25 had a hybrid phase of anatase and rutile TiO2. The diffraction peaks of P25 at 25.4°, 37.9°, 48.1°, 54.1°, 55.1°, 62.7°, 68.9°, 70.3° and 75.0° were easily labeled as the (1 0 1), (0 0 4), (2 0 0), (1 0 5), (2 1 1), (2 0 4), (1 1 6), (2 2 0) and (2 1 5) planes of anatase TiO2 (PDF#21-1272), while the diffraction peak at 27.5° was marked as the (1 1 0) plane of rutile TiO2 (PDF#21-1276) [49,50]. It was also found that the characteristic diffraction peaks between P25 and CDs/P25 remained unchanged. And no peaks of carbon species could be detected. This phenomenon was attributed to the relatively low content and weak diffraction intensity of CDs in the nanocomposites [51]. The diffraction peaks of CDs/Ag/P25 at 38.2°, 44.4°, 65.1° and 77.5° were labeled as the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) planes of Ag (PDF#04-0783), indicating the formation of AgNPs. Similarly, its other diffraction peaks were like that of P25. It was confirmed that the solvothermal preparation of CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25 had no influence on the crystalline phase of TiO2.

Raman spectra were used to confirm the presence of CDs in nanocomposite photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 3, all samples showed obvious characteristic peaks at 395 cm−1 (B1g), 514 cm−1 (A1g + B1g) and 636 cm−1 (Eg), which could be assigned to the anatase TiO2 phase. In addition to the peaks from TiO2, CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25 concurrently showed two additional D-band and G-band peaks at 1364 cm−1 and 1582 cm−1, respectively, which were the Raman-active modes of the CDs. The results illustrated the existence of CDs in both CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25.

The structures of photocatalysts were further confirmed by an inverted fluorescence microscopy. As shown in Fig. S1, the light images and the fluorescent images were well matched. P25 only exhibited obvious blue fluorescence under UV light excitation and no fluorescence under blue and green light excitation. Nevertheless, CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25 showed clear blue, green and red fluorescence under the same excitation conditions. Results showed that CDs had strong fluorescence and good dispersion. The fluorescence of CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25 were derived from CDs, which reconfirmed the presence of CDs in these nanocomposites.

As shown in Fig. 4, the morphological features and elemental composition of photocatalysts were investigated by SEM, TEM and EDS analysis. The SEM images proved that both CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25 possessed spherical shapes with an average diameter of 30 nm. The distributions of CDs and AgNPs could not be observed due to the small size of CDs and the low content of AgNPs. The TEM images showed the presence of CDs and AgNPs in CDs/Ag/P25. The large particles with a diameter of 30–50 nm were identified as TiO2 nanoparticles and AgNPs, while the surrounding smaller particles with average size of 3–5 nm were considered as CDs (marked by circles). The lattice spacing of about 0.35 nm and 0.24 nm were caused by the (1 0 1) plane of anatase TiO2 and the (2 2 0) plane of crystalline Ag, respectively, coinciding with the XRD results. But the lattice spacing of CDs could not be observed. The possible reason was that the synthesis process of CDs/Ag/P25 caused the reduction in the lattice structure of CDs. The EDS spectrum of CDs/Ag/P25 proved the presence of O, Ti, Ag and C elements. And as shown in Fig. S2, the EDS elemental maps of CDs/Ag/P25 confirmed the elements of O, Ti, Ag and C evenly distributed in the CDs/Ag/P25. Therefore, it could be concluded that CDs/Ag/P25 photocatalyst was composed of CDs, AgNPs and TiO2 nanoparticles. And AgNPs and CDs were tightly anchored on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. The intimate interface combinations between CDs, AgNPs and TiO2 nanoparticles were beneficial to the transfer of the photogenerated electrons between them, leading to enhancing the photocatalytic activity of CDs/Ag/P25 in turn.

XPS analysis was measured to detect the elemental component and chemical states of CDs/Ag/P25. The full XPS spectra ( Fig. 5a) showed the presence of O, Ti, Ag and C elements in CDs/Ag/P25, which was also consistent with the EDS result. The atomic percentages of O, Ti, Ag and C were 38.89%, 19.64%, 0.39% and 41.09%, respectively. Although the Ag content was low, it played an important role in the photocatalytic process. The peaks at 366.2 eV and 372.2 eV corresponded to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 5b). The spin energy separation was 6.0 eV, illuminating that Ag had a metallic nature [52]. As shown in the Ti 2p spectra (Fig. S3), the peaks at 457.9 eV and 463.6 eV belonged to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, separately. The spin energy separation was 5.7 eV, indicating an oxidation state of Ti4+ [53]. Fig. 5c-d showed the C 1s and O 1s spectra. The peaks at 284 eV, 285.1 eV and 288.1 eV for C 1s spectra could be assigned to C-C, C-O and C˭O bonds, respectively. And the peaks at 529.1 eV, 530.2 eV and 530.8 eV for O 1s spectra could be attributed to the Ti-O, C˭O and C-O bonds, separately. In general, the existence of interfacial bonding between CDs and TiO2 could benefit the migration of photogenerated electrons [54,55]. The C 1s and O 1s spectra indicated the presence of carbon–oxygen bonds between CDs and TiO2 nanoparticles, which was beneficial to the enhancement of photocatalytic activity. Notably, there were no peaks of Ti-C bond in both Ti 2p spectra and C 1s spectra. This demonstrated that CDs were anchored on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles instead of doping into the TiO2.

As one of the representative organic dyes, MB has been widely used in printing, dyeing and leather industries [56]. From the Fig. S4, it could be observed that the concentrations of MB solutions (2–20 mg/L) had a linear correlation with the characteristic absorption peaks at 664 nm. In our study, the photo-degradation of MB was carried out under UV and visible light to measure the photocatalytic activities of photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 6, reactions followed pseudo-first-order kinetics in the studied concentration range [57]. P25 decomposed 80% of the MB dye in 15 min under UV light. But CDs/Ag/P25 almost decomposed all the MB dye within the same period. TiO2 with a band gap of 3.2 eV could not be excited to generate electron-hole pairs under visible light, thus P25 only decomposed 5% of the MB dye in 2 h. However, CDs/Ag/P25 could decompose 20% of the MB dye with the introduction of CDs and AgNPs, and its degradation rate was 4 times higher than that of P25. Safardoust et al. [58] reported that 0.1 g N-GQDs (N-Doped Graphene Quantum Dots) / TiO2 nanocomposite degraded 85% of MB (10 mg/L) within 70 min under the condition that 400 W UV tube was irradiated. Compared to this nanocomposite, CDs/Ag/P25 in the current study exhibited great photocatalytic performance in MB degradation (100% degradation within 15 min) under UV light. Moreover, CDs/Ag/P25 also degraded MB under visible light. Although the photocatalytic activity of CDs/Ag/P25 under visible light was much lower than that under UV light, it provided a new way for TiO2 to make full use of the solar energy. In conclusion, no matter what light source used, CDs/Ag/P25 exhibited the enhanced photocatalytic performance compared with P25 and CDs/P25.

It was important to investigate the roles of CDs and AgNPs in CDs/Ag/P25 photocatalyst. To reveal the functions of the photocatalysts, their diffuse reflectance spectra were studied in detail. As shown in Fig. 7, P25 only exhibited the fundamental absorption band in the UV light region and no more absorption in the visible light region [59]. The absorption intensity of CDs/P25 was much stronger than that of P25 at the wavelength of 400–700 nm, indicating that CDs could affect the optical property of P25. As for CDs/Ag/P25, the introduction of AgNPs had little effect on the absorption of UV light, but it could significantly improve the absorption of visible light, especially in the range from 400 nm to 500 nm. And the absorption peak in the range of 440–460 nm corresponded to the SPR effect of AgNPs [60]. It was found that CDs/Ag/P25 had the highest visible light absorption among these photocatalysts. The reason was that both the photoelectrical properties of CDs and the SPR effect of AgNPs could promote the absorption of visible light. Notably, there were nearly no differences for the band gaps between P25, CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25. It reconfirmed that CDs were attached on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles instead of doping into the TiO2, coinciding with the results from the XPS characterization. As shown in the inset picture, the whiteness of the powders decreased from P25 to CDs/P25, and then the color changed to brownish yellow after the addition of AgNPs.

In addition to the light adsorption, surface area was also critical to photocatalytic activity. Fig. S5 showed the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of P25, CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25. Results revealed that all curves coincided with a type II isotherm with nonporous [61]. As shown in Table S1, the calculated BET results of P25, CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25 were 79.1, 57.5 and 62.5 m2/g, respectively. The specific surface areas of CDs/P25 and CDs/Ag/P25 only had a slight reduction compared with that of P25 due to the solvothermal synthesis of photocatalysts. It was concluded that the change of the specific surface area could not affect the improved photocatalytic activities of these photocatalysts.

As shown in Fig. 8, the PL spectra of all the samples were carried out to explore the electron transfer. The lower PL intensity indicated the higher separation efficiency. Compared with pure P25, the PL intensities of CDs /P25 and CDs/Ag/P25 significant decreased. In particular, CDs/Ag/P25 showed the lowest PL intensity in all samples, indicating that it had the most efficient separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes under UV light. It was due to the electrons transfer between TiO2, CDs and AgNPs, thus preventing direct recombination of electrons and holes.

In order to probe the main active species of CDs/Ag/P25, a series of experiments with the addition of different free radical scavengers were measured to quench •OH, h+ and •O2 −, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9, the photo-degradation rate only slightly decreased with the addition of BQ as the •O2 − scavenger. It revealed that •O2 − played a crucial role during the photo-degradation process. However, the degradation of MB was significantly suppressed with the addition of IPA and AO as the •OH and h+ scavenger, separately, suggesting that •OH and h+ were not important for the degradation of MB. Therefore, it was concluded that the main active specie for the excellent photocatalytic activity of CDs/Ag/P25 was •O2 −.

Based on the above experimental results and literature references, the possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of CDs/Ag/P25 was illustrated in Fig. 10. Firstly, the electrons in CDs can be easily transferred to the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles and AgNPs, and the extra electrons on TiO2 nanoparticles and AgNPs can also be transferred to CDs. The unique photoelectrical properties of CDs can increase the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Moreover, TiO2 can work as a photosensitizer to generate electron-hole pairs under UV light. Because of the intense SPR effect, AgNPs can also be stimulated to produce electron-hole pairs under visible light. After producing electron-hole pairs, the holes react to form •OH to degrade the pollutants. Meanwhile, the electrons transfer between CDs, AgNPs and TiO2 nanoparticles, and then react with O2 to degrade the pollutants. These aspects together illustrate the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/Ag/P25.

In summary, CDs/Ag/P25 ternary nanocomposites consisting of CDs, AgNPs and TiO2 nanoparticles, have been synthesized by one-pot solvothermal method. The results of XRD, SEM and TEM revealed the newborn AgNPs and CDs were tightly anchored on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles instead of doping into the TiO2. And the crystalline phase of TiO2 nanoparticles remained unchanged. The XPS analysis indicated the presence of carbon–oxygen bonds between CDs and TiO2 nanoparticles, which was beneficial to the transfer of the photogenerated electrons. Compared with P25 and CDs/P25, CDs/Ag/P25 exhibited much higher photocatalytic performance under UV and visible light. The photoelectrical properties of CDs and the SPR effect of AgNPs could both enhance the absorption of visible light and hinder the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The present work can provide a novel insight for the facile fabrication of highly effective ternary photocatalysts, which own great potential for the future applications in wastewater treatment and environmental protection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Sciences Foundation of China (No. 51502345) and Tianjin Sciences Foundation (16JCQNJC03100).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ceramint.2018.09.017.
